ocar.opportunity: Volume electron microscopy techniques for 3D ultrastructural imaging of biological specimens had matured significantly from specialized research tools to broadly applicable methods, but a comprehensive review of all current approaches and best practices for biologists was lacking.
ocar.challenge: The diversity of volume EM approaches (SBEM, FIB-SEM, ATUM-SEM, EDT, array tomography) had distinct strengths and weaknesses that required deep technical expertise to navigate; and specimen preparation protocols were still being optimized for different biological questions.
ocar.action: Peddie and Collinson provide a comprehensive review of volume EM techniques from a practitioner's perspective, covering specimen preparation methods (HPF/FS, chemical fixation, heavy metal staining), all major imaging modalities (SBEM, FIB-SEM, serial section TEM/SEM, array tomography), and data analysis workflows. They include practical guidance on instrument selection, acquisition parameters, and representative applications in cell biology and neuroscience.
ocar.resolution: The review demonstrates that volume EM has transitioned from a specialist technique to an accessible tool enabling 3D ultrastructural investigation of diverse biological systems. SBEM and FIB-SEM are positioned as complementary: SBEM for larger volumes at moderate resolution, FIB-SEM for smaller volumes at isotropic nanometer resolution. High-pressure freezing/freeze substitution (HPF/FS) is established as the gold standard for ultrastructural preservation.
ocar.future_work: Increasing throughput of FIB-SEM systems to approach SBEM volumes, developing better correlative workflows linking molecular labeling to ultrastructure, and improving automated analysis for non-neural biological applications remained active development areas.

summaries.beginner: This review explains how scientists have developed multiple ways to take detailed 3D photographs of cells at the nanometer scale using electron microscopes. It's a practical guide explaining the different approaches, their advantages, and how to choose the right technique for a specific biological question.
summaries.intermediate: Peddie and Collinson review volume EM techniques comprehensively, comparing HPF versus chemical fixation for ultrastructural preservation, SBEM versus FIB-SEM versus serial section TEM versus array tomography for different volume/resolution requirements, and discussing application examples from organelle biology, cell division, and neural circuit reconstruction. They provide practical guidance on instrument setup, acquisition parameters, and data management.
summaries.advanced: This review captures the maturing state of volume EM in 2014, providing a practitioner-oriented complement to the more neuroscience-focused Briggman & Bock (2011) review. Key contributions include a systematic comparison of specimen preparation protocols with ultrastructural quality metrics, and a practical framework for selecting imaging modalities based on volume requirements and resolution needs. The discussion of HPF/FS advantages for preserving membrane ultrastructure and cytoskeletal organization is directly relevant to connectomics tissue preparation quality—a factor that significantly influences automated segmentation performance.

discussion_prompts: What are the key advantages of high-pressure freezing/freeze substitution over chemical fixation for preserving ultrastructure in volume EM, and when are chemical fixation protocols still preferred?
How do the throughput and resolution characteristics of SBEM versus FIB-SEM determine their respective application spaces in biological research?
What are the main sources of imaging artifacts in volume EM, and how do they affect automated segmentation and circuit reconstruction?
